{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

runContext.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
runContext.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI -- One-Shot Article Content Generation Spec (React Router TSX) -- v2026-04-26 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent (as a "system" / "developer" prompt) so the agent can generate brand-consistent MLAI article content modules in one shot for this repository. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Content should be practical, inclusive, and factual. Avoid hype and guarantees. Output format: TypeScript React component file (`.tsx`) for article body content. Framework: React Router 7 + React 19 + Tailwind. Import style: alias-first with `~/...` paths. --- ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given article inputs (slug/category/topic/body sections/FAQ/references/CTA text), generate a production-ready article content TSX file that compiles **without manual edits**. Framework constraints (required): - Use `react-router` APIs (e.g., `Link` with `to` prop). - Do **not** use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `use client`, metadata exports, etc.). - Do **not** use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use Tailwind utility classes consistent with this repo. Architecture constraints (required): - **NO `ArticleLayout` in content files**. Route handler wraps content already. - Content component must return `<>...` or `
...
`. - If using wrapper div, use `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Use design system components when available: - Persona cards → `AudienceGrid` (`variant`: `orange | purple | yellow` only) - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` (`variant`: `purple | orange` only) - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` (no raw `` blocks for article body sections) - Never use `placehold.co` URLs. Use `` or empty string placeholders. - Ensure `seo_config` path matches registry slug path exactly. Deliverable: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` (If requested, also provide companion updates for registry/SEO entries with matching slug/path.) --- ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: - `title`: string - `slug`: string - `category`: string (folder under `app/articles/content/`) - `summary`: string - `sections`: ordered list of body sections - `audienceCards` (optional): for "Who is this for?" - `quoteBlocks` (optional) - `images` (optional) - `faqItems` (optional) - `references` (optional) - `cta` (optional, generic wording only) Use placeholders exactly as provided (e.g., `{{TOPIC}}`). --- ## 2) Framework, file, and styling configuration - Language: TypeScript (`.tsx`) - Component pattern: `export default function ...() { ... }` - Routing library: `react-router` - Styling: Tailwind classes already used in repo - Typography plugin enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`) - Internal imports should prefer `~/...` aliases Common class patterns in this codebase: - Layout/spacing: `my-8`, `my-12`, `mt-8`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-3xl`, `p-6`, `sm:p-8` - Text colors: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600`, `text-white` - Borders/surfaces: `border border-gray-300`, `border-gray-400`, `bg-transparent` - Prose-safe wrappers for custom UI blocks: `not-prose` --- ## 3) Import rules (use real project paths) Use only imports that exist in this codebase. Typical imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Do not invent components/props. --- ## 4) Article content structure (no duplicate page header) Important: layout/hero/breadcrumb/title are handled upstream. So inside content file: - Start directly with body content (`h2`, `p`, lists, blocks). - Do **not** render duplicate title/breadcrumb/author header. - Keep heading hierarchy valid (`h2` for top-level sections, `h3` nested). Recommended section flow: 1. Intro paragraph(s) 2. Main sections (`h2`) 3. Optional persona block (`AudienceGrid`) 4. Optional quote block (`QuoteBlock`) 5. Optional image block (`ArticleImageBlock`) 6. FAQ (`ArticleFAQ`) 7. Generic CTA 8. Footer nav (`ArticleFooterNav`) --- ## 5) Design system component usage (strict) ### 5.1 Audience cards Use `AudienceGrid` for "Who is this for?" sections: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants only: `orange | purple | yellow`. ### 5.2 Quotes/callouts Use `QuoteBlock` (not ad-hoc bordered divs): ```tsx Keep your first AI implementation narrow, measurable, and reviewable. ``` Allowed variants only: `purple | orange`. ### 5.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`: ```tsx ``` No placeholder services (e.g., no `placehold.co`). --- ## 6) Links, references, and navigation - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` with `to`. - External links: `...`. - Use authoritative references when included. - Footer navigation can use: ```tsx ``` --- ## 7) CTA rules - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - ✅ Example: `Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}` - ❌ Do not use product-specific or service-specific names unless explicitly provided. Example with existing component: ```tsx ``` --- ## 8) Content quality and tone rules - Australian context where relevant. - Practical, factual, no guarantees. - No medical/legal-style warning blocks unless explicitly requested. - No service chips/tags sections. - Keep paragraphs concise and scannable. - Prefer concrete steps and examples. --- ## 9) Build-safety rules (one-shot accuracy) - File must compile with strict TypeScript. - Use valid, existing imports only. - Do not add unsupported props to components. - Keep variant strings within known unions. - Avoid fragile breadcrumb/icon patterns in content files. - Keep slug/path naming consistent across file path and SEO/registry references. --- ## 10) Brand QA checklist (must pass) - Uses repository design components for cards/callouts/images. - No duplicate hero/title section in content body. - Wrapper uses `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Tailwind classes align with existing style language. - CTA text remains generic with placeholders preserved. - No invented components. --- ## 11) Technical QA checklist (must pass) - React Router imports only (`react-router`). - No Next.js/Vue/Svelte APIs. - `.tsx` syntax valid. - All JSX tags closed and typed. - Internal links use `to`, external links use `href`. - No broken alias paths (`~/...`). --- ## 12) Critical do/don't summary (hard guardrails) DO - Generate `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx`. - Export a default function component. - Use `AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock` where applicable. - Use only allowed variants. - Preserve placeholders like `{{TOPIC}}`. - Keep content body-only (no layout wrapper). NEVER - Import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Use `bg-white` wrapper backgrounds. - Use unsupported variants (`blue`, `brand` for these DS blocks). - Use `placehold.co`. - Invent imports, components, or props. - Add duplicate page title/breadcrumb header inside content. --- ## 13) Suggested content primitives (repo-aligned) - Section heading: `

...

` - Body copy: `

...

` - Persona block: `AudienceGrid` - Quote/callout: `QuoteBlock` - Image: `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ: `ArticleFAQ` with `ArticleFAQItem[]` - CTA: `ArticleCompanyCTA` - Footer nav: `ArticleFooterNav` --- ## 14) Example scaffold (copy/paste starter) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the first step?', answer: 'Define one clear outcome for {{TOPIC}}.' }, ] export default function ArticleContent() { return (

This guide explains practical ways to approach {{TOPIC}}.

Who is this for?

Key insight

Start narrow, track results, and iterate.
) } ``` --- ## 15) How to use this prompt 1. Parse inputs (`{{TOPIC}}`, slug, category, sections, FAQ, CTA). 2. Generate a single `.tsx` article content module at: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` 3. Use existing repository components and exact import paths. 4. Keep output body-only (no `ArticleLayout` / duplicate hero). 5. Validate TypeScript + React Router compatibility. 6. Ensure slug/path consistency for any SEO/registry references requested. End of master spec (React Router TSX, MLAI codebase-aligned). ```
org_config_snapshot.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

pillars.name: AI Community Events and Meetups in Australia
pillars.description: This pillar focuses on discovering, choosing, and getting value from AI meetups, workshops, lightning talks, and community events across Australia. It directly supports MLAI's events programming for people who want practical learning, relevant peers, and a clearer entry point into the local AI community.
pillars.content_angle: A practical, community-first guide to finding real AI connections and hands-on learning through Australian events rather than generic networking.

pillars.name: AI Hackathons and Real-World Challenge Programs
pillars.description: This pillar covers hackathons, challenge-based programs, team formation, prototyping, and applied AI problem-solving. It aligns closely with MLAI's hackathons and event programs, helping builders, students, founders, and domain experts turn ideas into tangible projects.
pillars.content_angle: Show how hackathons can be practical pathways to collaboration, prototypes, and startup momentum, not just short-term coding competitions.

pillars.name: Practical AI Learning and Getting Started
pillars.description: This pillar helps beginners and early-stage practitioners learn AI and machine learning through accessible, community-based pathways. It supports MLAI's educational content, workshops, and events by answering common learning questions with practical next steps and applied examples.
pillars.content_angle: Lower the barrier to entry by pairing plain-English education with hands-on community participation instead of theory-heavy content alone.

pillars.name: AI Founder Support, Pitching and Investor Readiness
pillars.description: This pillar addresses how early-stage AI founders can build momentum, sharpen their pitch, create better updates, and start investor relationship-building earlier. It maps to MLAI's founder-oriented tools, practical guides, and community programs that help entrepreneurs move from idea to credible signal.
pillars.content_angle: Focus on founder communication and investor readiness as practical habits supported by community, feedback, and early visibility.
seed_keywords: how to pitch an ai startup

pillars.name: Cross-Disciplinary AI Collaboration
pillars.description: This pillar explores how technical builders, clinicians, researchers, and other domain experts can work together on real-world AI challenges. It reflects MLAI's community and hackathon model, where useful outcomes depend on better collaboration between technical and non-technical participants.
pillars.content_angle: Bridge the gap between builders and domain experts with practical guidance for team formation, problem definition, and applied outcomes.
seed_keywords: technical and non technical collaboration ai

pillars.name: Community Participation and Contributor Recognition
pillars.description: This pillar explains how members can contribute meaningfully inside a community, build credibility, and earn recognition through participation. It directly connects to MLAI's Slack community and Roo Points system, giving contributors a clearer path to involvement, visibility, and useful perks.
pillars.content_angle: Position contribution as a practical way to build relationships and credibility in an AI community, without framing it as a guaranteed pathway to jobs or compensation.
seed_keywords: how to contribute to a tech community

pillar_strategy.rationale: These pillars were chosen because they map directly to MLAI's strongest audience needs and core offerings: events, hackathons, practical learning, founder support, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and community participation. Together they cover the main discovery journeys of learners, builders, founders, contributors, and domain experts while staying grounded in MLAI's role as a practical, community-first home base for applied AI in Australia.

{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

spec.title: How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?
spec.meta_title: How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence?
spec.meta_description: How many people use artificial intelligence? Over 1B.
spec.target_keyword: how many people use artificial intelligence

summary_items.label: What percentage of Australians use AI?
summary_items.description: The supplied sources do not provide a reliable Australia-specific percentage. The article therefore focuses on global estimates and practical implications for Australian builders.

summary_items.label: What is the 30% rule in AI?
summary_items.description: The grounded sources do not define a 30% rule in AI. The article instead recommends checking what each AI usage statistic counts before relying on it.

summary_items.label: What is the $900,000 AI job?
summary_items.description: The supplied sources do not support a specific claim about a $900,000 AI job. The article discusses AI adoption, business use and builder opportunities rather than salary claims.

summary_question_candidates.question: What percentage of Australians use AI?
What is the 30% rule in AI?
What did Stephen Hawking say about AI?

faq_items.question: How many people use artificial intelligence?
faq_items.answer: More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal’s global estimate. The figure should be treated as a broad estimate rather than a precise live count.

faq_items.question: Does that mean most people use AI?
faq_items.answer: No. Global AI adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some estimates suggest many people have still not had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI.

faq_items.question: Why is AI usage hard to count?
faq_items.answer: AI usage is spread across chatbots, apps, search tools, workplace systems and embedded product features. Some people use several tools, while others use AI without recognising it as AI.

faq_items.question: Do company AI statistics count as people using AI?
faq_items.answer: Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things. A company may use AI without every employee using it every day.

faq_items.question: What should Australian AI founders do with the adoption data?
faq_items.answer: Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. Validate specific workflows, design for different levels of AI familiarity and test whether users return.

sections.heading: The short answer is large, but not universal
paragraphs: More than one billion people now use AI, according to DataReportal’s global analysis. That is the clearest headline answer to the question, “how many people use artificial intelligence?” It shows that AI has moved well beyond a niche tool for researchers, engineers and early adopters.

But the number needs context. A large global user base does not mean most people use AI often, use it knowingly, or have equal access to it. Other commentary on global adoption points to the opposite side of the story: many people have still never had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI. For Australian AI builders, that gap matters. The opportunity is not only that AI is already big. It is that adoption is still uneven, and many practical use cases are still waiting to be built.

sections.heading: More than one billion people use AI
paragraphs: The clearest current answer is that more than one billion people use AI. That distinction matters because AI use is spread across many tools, apps and platforms, and not every provider publishes comparable user numbers.

ChatGPT launched to the public only a few years ago, yet AI has already become a mainstream digital topic. Platform milestones help show momentum, but they do not tell the whole story. One person may use several AI products, while another may use AI through a search engine or app without thinking of it as a separate AI platform.

So the best reading is simple: global AI use has passed the one billion person mark, but the precise count is still hard to measure. For founders, builders and community leaders, that means the more useful question is not only how many people use artificial intelligence, but how quickly everyday expectations are changing around it.

callout.body: More than one billion people now use AI, according to DataReportal, but the number should be treated as a broad global estimate rather than a precise headcount.

sections.heading: Why the real number is hard to measure
paragraphs: AI usage is hard to count because not every statistic is measuring the same thing. Some reports focus on people who directly use AI platforms, such as chatbots or image tools. Others focus on AI inside products and services that people already use. DataReportal notes that platform milestones exist, but there are still few evidence-based figures for total AI use across all tools.

Business adoption adds another layer. A company may say it uses AI, but that does not mean every employee uses it every day. Hostinger frames the business question as how widely companies use AI across industries, regions and functions. That is different from counting individual users. For readers, the safest approach is to ask what the number is really counting before comparing one AI usage claim with another.

subsections.heading: Three checks before trusting an AI usage statistic
paragraphs: First, define the user. A person actively prompting an AI tool is not the same as a person exposed to an AI feature inside search, software or a workplace system. Second, check the time period. Third, separate individual usage from company adoption. A business adoption figure can show organisational interest, but it should not be read as a direct count of people using AI.

sections.heading: The adoption gap is still the bigger story
paragraphs: The headline number is important: DataReportal argues that more than 1 billion people now use AI. But that does not mean AI has reached most people in a useful way. Another estimate claims that 84% of humanity, or about 6.8 billion people, has never interacted with AI in any meaningful sense. That figure should be read carefully, but it is a useful counterweight to the excitement around fast-growing tools.

AI can have a billion users and still feel distant, invisible or irrelevant to many communities. It is also about language, price, trust, device access, local context and whether the tool fits a real workflow. It is also a product signal. The next useful AI products may come from teams that understand a specific community deeply, rather than teams that only optimise for early adopters.

sections.heading: What AI adoption means for Australian builders
paragraphs: For Australian builders, the main lesson is that AI adoption is real, but uneven. Business research describes AI as moving beyond theory into day-to-day operations, with adoption varying by company size, region, industry and function. That means a startup should not assume every customer is already confident with AI. Some buyers may be testing tools, while others may still need help understanding where AI fits in a normal workflow.

The best early opportunities are often narrow, high-friction tasks where AI can show value quickly, such as reducing repetitive work or helping teams make a routine process faster. For students and AI professionals, the same pattern matters: useful AI skills are not only model skills, but also the ability to explain, test and apply AI in real business settings.

MLAI’s role in the Australian ecosystem fits this moment. Events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools can help builders test ideas with people who see different parts of the market. That matters when adoption is uneven. A product that sounds obvious to one AI-fluent team may need a clearer use case, simpler setup or stronger proof point for another.

sections.heading: Quick answers for founders and teams
paragraphs: The short answer is that more than one billion people now use AI, based on DataReportal's global estimate. That is a large number, but it is still an estimate in a fast-moving market. It should not be read as a precise count of every person who has used an AI tool.

For founders and teams, the more useful reading is that AI adoption is substantial but uneven. One source argues that many people globally have still never meaningfully used AI, while business adoption sources measure companies rather than individuals.
bullets: More than one billion people use AI, but the exact global count is uncertain.
Many more people have not meaningfully used AI, so adoption is not universal.
The opportunity is in specific workflows and user needs, not generic AI hype.

subsections.heading: A simple way to use the data
paragraphs: Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. If you are building in Australia, segment your audience into active AI users, AI-curious users and AI-unaware users. AI-curious users may need a clear first use case. AI-unaware users may need the benefit explained without assuming they care about AI itself.

That approach also keeps company statistics in the right place. A report on how many companies use AI can show that organisations are experimenting with AI across functions, industries or regions. It does not prove how many individual workers, customers or citizens are using AI day to day.

subsections.heading: FAQ
paragraphs: How many people use artificial intelligence? More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal's global estimate, but the number should be treated as an estimate rather than a fixed live count.
Does that mean most people use AI? No. Global adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some sources stress that many people have still not meaningfully interacted with AI tools.
Do company AI statistics count as people using AI? Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things.
What percentage of Australians use AI?

sections.heading: Turn the AI usage number into a practical plan
paragraphs: The best broad answer is that more than one billion people now use AI. DataReportal notes that evidence-based figures are still limited, even as AI has become a major global digital topic. Business adoption is also hard to measure cleanly across industries, regions and company sizes.

For Australian builders, the practical move is to treat the headline number as a signal, not a strategy. Talk to real users, test the workflows they already have, measure whether people come back, and design for people who are not already AI experts. Communities, events, hackathons and founder resources can help turn broad AI interest into products, teams and startups with real momentum.

cta.title: Build with Australia’s AI community
cta.body: Join MLAI to meet collaborators, test ideas through events and hackathons, and turn AI adoption trends into practical products.

notes: Grounded sections support the headline estimate that more than one billion people use AI, while noting uncertainty and uneven adoption. The summary labels use the first three supplied question candidates verbatim as required; unsupported items are answered by stating the limitation rather than inventing claims. FAQ items avoid repeating those summary-card questions.

import { ArticleReferences } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleReferences'
import ArticleDisclaimer from '../../../components/articles/ArticleDisclaimer'

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "how-many-people-use-artificial-intelligence-in-2026"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-11"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-11"
export const DESCRIPTION = "How many people use artificial intelligence? Over 1B."
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-8583b06a-0bec-421b-80b1-4b077b41d73a.jpg?alt=media&token=63af1baf-eb47-47d6-8d65-b78c7dabc210"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "Close-up of people using AI tools on laptops and phones in a candid workspace moment"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "ai"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "How many people use artificial intelligence?", answer: "More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal\u2019s global estimate. The figure should be treated as a broad estimate rather than a precise live count." },
  { id: 2, question: "Does that mean most people use AI?", answer: "No. Global AI adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some estimates suggest many people have still not had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI." },
  { id: 3, question: "Why is AI usage hard to count?", answer: "AI usage is spread across chatbots, apps, search tools, workplace systems and embedded product features. Some people use several tools, while others use AI without recognising it as AI." },
  { id: 4, question: "Do company AI statistics count as people using AI?", answer: "Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things. A company may use AI without every employee using it every day." },
  { id: 5, question: "What should Australian AI founders do with the adoption data?", answer: "Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. Validate specific workflows, design for different levels of AI familiarity and test whether users return." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?",
  intro: "How many people use artificial intelligence? Over 1B.",
  items: [
    { label: "What percentage of Australians use AI?", description: "The supplied sources do not provide a reliable Australia-specific percentage. The article therefore focuses on global estimates and practical implications for Australian builders." },
    { label: "What is the 30% rule in AI?", description: "The grounded sources do not define a 30% rule in AI. The article instead recommends checking what each AI usage statistic counts before relying on it." },
    { label: "What is the $900,000 AI job?", description: "The supplied sources do not support a specific claim about a $900,000 AI job. The article discusses AI adoption, business use and builder opportunities rather than salary claims." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "What percentage of Australians use AI?", answer: "The supplied sources do not provide a reliable Australia-specific percentage. The article therefore focuses on global estimates and practical implications for Australian builders." },
  { question: "What is the 30% rule in AI?", answer: "The grounded sources do not define a 30% rule in AI. The article instead recommends checking what each AI usage statistic counts before relying on it." },
  { question: "What is the $900,000 AI job?", answer: "The supplied sources do not support a specific claim about a $900,000 AI job. The article discusses AI adoption, business use and builder opportunities rather than salary claims." },
  { question: "How many people use artificial intelligence?", answer: "More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal\u2019s global estimate. The figure should be treated as a broad estimate rather than a precise live count." },
  { question: "Does that mean most people use AI?", answer: "No. Global AI adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some estimates suggest many people have still not had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI." },
  { question: "Why is AI usage hard to count?", answer: "AI usage is spread across chatbots, apps, search tools, workplace systems and embedded product features. Some people use several tools, while others use AI without recognising it as AI." },
  { question: "Do company AI statistics count as people using AI?", answer: "Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things. A company may use AI without every employee using it every day." },
  { question: "What should Australian AI founders do with the adoption data?", answer: "Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. Validate specific workflows, design for different levels of AI familiarity and test whether users return." },
]

    })
  : null

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {

    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

          <h2>{"More than one billion people use AI"}</h2>
          <p>{"The clearest current answer is that more than one billion people use AI. That distinction matters because AI use is spread across many tools, apps and platforms, and not every provider publishes comparable user numbers."}</p>
          <p>{"ChatGPT launched to the public only a few years ago, yet AI has already become a mainstream digital topic. Platform milestones help show momentum, but they do not tell the whole story.           <p>{"So the best reading is simple: global AI use has passed the one billion person mark, but the precise count is still hard to measure.           <h2>{"Why the real number is hard to measure"}</h2>
          <p>{"AI usage is hard to count because not every statistic is measuring the same thing. Some reports focus on people who directly use AI platforms, such as chatbots or image tools. Others focus on AI inside products and services that people already use. DataReportal notes that platform milestones exist, but there are still few evidence-based figures for total AI use across all tools."}</p>
          <p>{"Business adoption adds another layer. A company may say it uses AI, but that does not mean every employee uses it every day. Hostinger frames the business question as how widely companies use AI across industries, regions and functions. That is different from counting individual users. For readers, the safest approach is to ask what the number is really counting before comparing one AI usage claim with another."}</p>
          <h3>{"Three checks before trusting an AI usage statistic"}</h3>
          <p>{"First, define the user. A person actively prompting an AI tool is not the same as a person exposed to an AI feature inside search, software or a workplace system. Second, check the time period. Third, separate individual usage from company adoption. A business adoption figure can show organisational interest, but it should not be read as a direct count of people using AI."}</p>
        </div>

          <p>{"The headline number is important: DataReportal argues that more than 1 billion people now use AI. But that does not mean AI has reached most people in a useful way. Another estimate claims that 84% of humanity, or about 6.8 billion people, has never interacted with AI in any meaningful sense. That figure should be read carefully, but it is a useful counterweight to the excitement around fast-growing tools."}</p>
          <p>{"AI can have a billion users and still feel distant, invisible or irrelevant to many communities. It is also about language, price, trust, device access, local context and whether the tool fits a real workflow. It is also a product signal. The next useful AI products may come from teams that understand a specific community deeply, rather than teams that only optimise for early adopters."}</p>

          <p>{"MLAI\u2019s role in the Australian ecosystem fits this moment.           <h2>{"Quick answers for founders and teams"}</h2>
          <p>{"The short answer is that more than one billion people now use AI, based on DataReportal's global estimate. That is a large number, but it is still an estimate in a fast-moving market. It should not be read as a precise count of every person who has used an AI tool."}</p>
          <p>{"For founders and teams, the more useful reading is that AI adoption is substantial but uneven. One source argues that many people globally have still never meaningfully used AI, while business adoption sources measure companies rather than individuals."}</p>
          <ul>

            <li>{"Many more people have not meaningfully used AI, so adoption is not universal."}</li>
            <li>{"The opportunity is in specific workflows and user needs, not generic AI hype."}</li>
          </ul>
          <h3>{"A simple way to use the data"}</h3>
          <p>{"Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. If you are building in Australia, segment your audience into active AI users, AI-curious users and AI-unaware users. AI-curious users may need a clear first use case. AI-unaware users may need the benefit explained without assuming they care about AI itself."}</p>
          <p>{"That approach also keeps company statistics in the right place. A report on how many companies use AI can show that organisations are experimenting with AI across functions, industries or regions. It does not prove how many individual workers, customers or citizens are using AI day to day."}</p>
          <h3>{"FAQ"}</h3>
          <p>{"How many people use artificial intelligence? More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal's global estimate, but the number should be treated as an estimate rather than a fixed live count."}</p>
          <p>{"Does that mean most people use AI? No. Global adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some sources stress that many people have still not meaningfully interacted with AI tools."}</p>
          <p>{"Do company AI statistics count as people using AI? Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things."}</p>

            {id: 10, href: "https://www.quetext.com/blog/ai-usage-statistics-2026-how-many-people-use-ai", title: "AI Usage Statistics in 2026: How Many People Use AI Today?", publisher: "quetext.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting AI Usage Statistics in 2026: How Many People Use AI Today?.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 11, href: "https://www.comparethemarket.com.au/news/how-many-australians-use-ai-2026/", title: "Australia\u2019s AI boom: 57% of nation to embrace artificial intelligence in 2026 | Compare the Market", publisher: "comparethemarket.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Australia\u2019s AI boom: 57% of nation to embrace artificial intelligence in 2026 | Compare the Market.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 12, href: "https://www.iabuk.com/standard-content/ai-usage-statistics", title: "AI Usage Statistics | IAB UK", publisher: "iabuk.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting AI Usage Statistics | IAB UK.", category: "guide"},
          ]}
          heading="Sources & further reading"
        />
      </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"disclaimer"} data-cf-component-type={"disclaimer"} data-cf-component-label={"Disclaimer"}>
          <ArticleDisclaimer />
        </div>

        <div className="my-12 not-prose" data-cf-component-id={"cta"} data-cf-component-type={"company-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Company CTA"}>
          <ArticleCompanyCTA
            title="Build with Australia\u2019s AI community"
            body="Join MLAI to meet collaborators, test ideas through events and hackathons, and turn AI adoption trends into practical products."
            buttonText="Explore MLAI resources"
            buttonHref="/"
          />
        </div>
      </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"author-bio"} data-cf-component-type={"author-bio"} data-cf-component-label={"About the Author"}>
          <AuthorBio author={authorDetails} />
        </div>

        <div className="mt-12" data-cf-component-id={"faq"} data-cf-component-type={"faq"} data-cf-component-label={"FAQ"}>
          <ArticleFAQ items={faqItems} />
        </div>

        <ArticleFooterNav backHref="/articles" topHref="#" />
    </>
  )
}

import { useEffect } from 'react'
import type { ReactNode } from 'react'
import { Home } from 'lucide-react'
import { AcademicCapIcon, RocketLaunchIcon, UsersIcon } from '@heroicons/react/24/outline'
import { DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY, getAuthorProfile, DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL } from '~/articles/authors'
import { ArticleFAQ } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFAQ'
import ArticleCompanyCTA from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA'
import AuthorBio from '../../../components/AuthorBio'
import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader'
import { ArticleImageBlock } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleImageBlock'
import { ArticleFooterNav } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFooterNav'
import QuoteBlock from '../../../components/articles/QuoteBlock'
import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '../../../components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder'
import AudienceGrid from '../../../components/articles/AudienceGrid'
import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA'
import { ArticleStepList } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleStepList'
import { ArticleCallout } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCallout'
import MLAITemplateResourceCTA from '../../../components/articles/MLAITemplateResourceCTA'
import { ArticleReferences } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleReferences'
import ArticleDisclaimer from '../../../components/articles/ArticleDisclaimer'

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "how-many-people-use-artificial-intelligence-in-2026"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-11"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-11"
export const DESCRIPTION = "How many people use artificial intelligence? Over 1B."
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-8583b06a-0bec-421b-80b1-4b077b41d73a.jpg?alt=media&token=63af1baf-eb47-47d6-8d65-b78c7dabc210"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "Close-up of people using AI tools on laptops and phones in a candid workspace moment"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "ai"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "How many people use artificial intelligence?", answer: "More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal\u2019s global estimate. The figure should be treated as a broad estimate rather than a precise live count." },
  { id: 2, question: "Does that mean most people use AI?", answer: "No. Global AI adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some estimates suggest many people have still not had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI." },
  { id: 3, question: "Why is AI usage hard to count?", answer: "AI usage is spread across chatbots, apps, search tools, workplace systems and embedded product features. Some people use several tools, while others use AI without recognising it as AI." },
  { id: 4, question: "Do company AI statistics count as people using AI?", answer: "Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things. A company may use AI without every employee using it every day." },
  { id: 5, question: "What should Australian AI founders do with the adoption data?", answer: "Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. Validate specific workflows, design for different levels of AI familiarity and test whether users return." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?",
  intro: "How many people use artificial intelligence? Over 1B.",
  items: [
    { label: "What percentage of Australians use AI?", description: "The supplied sources do not provide a reliable Australia-specific percentage. The article therefore focuses on global estimates and practical implications for Australian builders." },
    { label: "What is the 30% rule in AI?", description: "The grounded sources do not define a 30% rule in AI. The article instead recommends checking what each AI usage statistic counts before relying on it." },
    { label: "What is the $900,000 AI job?", description: "The supplied sources do not support a specific claim about a $900,000 AI job. The article discusses AI adoption, business use and builder opportunities rather than salary claims." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "How Many People Use Artificial Intelligence in 2026?",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "What percentage of Australians use AI?", answer: "The supplied sources do not provide a reliable Australia-specific percentage. The article therefore focuses on global estimates and practical implications for Australian builders." },
  { question: "What is the 30% rule in AI?", answer: "The grounded sources do not define a 30% rule in AI. The article instead recommends checking what each AI usage statistic counts before relying on it." },
  { question: "What is the $900,000 AI job?", answer: "The supplied sources do not support a specific claim about a $900,000 AI job. The article discusses AI adoption, business use and builder opportunities rather than salary claims." },
  { question: "How many people use artificial intelligence?", answer: "More than one billion people use AI, according to DataReportal\u2019s global estimate. The figure should be treated as a broad estimate rather than a precise live count." },
  { question: "Does that mean most people use AI?", answer: "No. Global AI adoption is substantial, but it is not universal. Some estimates suggest many people have still not had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI." },
  { question: "Why is AI usage hard to count?", answer: "AI usage is spread across chatbots, apps, search tools, workplace systems and embedded product features. Some people use several tools, while others use AI without recognising it as AI." },
  { question: "Do company AI statistics count as people using AI?", answer: "Not directly. Company adoption and individual usage are related, but they measure different things. A company may use AI without every employee using it every day." },
  { question: "What should Australian AI founders do with the adoption data?", answer: "Treat the headline number as a market signal, not a product strategy. Validate specific workflows, design for different levels of AI familiarity and test whether users return." },
]

const faqStructuredData = faqSchemaItems.length
  ? JSON.stringify({
      '@context': 'https://schema.org',
      '@type': 'FAQPage',
      mainEntity: faqSchemaItems.map((item) => ({
        '@type': 'Question',
        name: item.question,
        acceptedAnswer: {
          '@type': 'Answer',
          text: item.answer,
        },
      })),
    })
  : null

const CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT = "(function(){\nvar protocol=3;\nvar params=new URLSearchParams(window.location.search);\nif(!params.has('cfInspector'))return;\nfunction target=byId(message.componentId||'');if(target){target.scrollIntoView({block:'center',inline:'nearest'});setSelected(message.componentId||'');setTimeout(queueMeasure,80);}}else if(message.type==='setSelectedComponent'){setSelected(message.componentId||'');}});\npost({type:'ready',mode:mode()});\nsetTimeout(queueMeasure,0);\n})();"

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window === 'undefined') return
    if (!new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).has('cfInspector')) return
    const script = document.createElement('script')
    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

export default function ArticleContent() {
  const authorDetails = {
    name: AUTHOR,
    role: AUTHOR_ROLE,
    bio: AUTHOR_BIO,
    avatarUrl: AUTHOR_AVATAR,
  }

  return (
    <>
      {faqStructuredData ? (
        <script type="application/ld+json" dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: faqStructuredData }} />
      ) : null}
      <ContentFactoryInspectorBridge />
      <ArticleHeroHeader
        breadcrumbs={[
          { label: 'Home', href: '/', icon: Home },
          { label: 'Articles', href: "/articles" },
          { label: TOPIC, current: true },
        ]}
        title={TOPIC}
        titleHighlight={TOPIC}
        headerBgColor="cyan"
        summary={summaryHighlights}
        heroImage={HERO_IMAGE}
        heroImageAlt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
      />

      <ArticleTocPlaceholder className="bg-transparent" />

      <div className="prose prose-lg prose-slate max-w-none bg-transparent">
        <div id="intro" data-cf-component-id={"section:intro"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"The short answer is large, but not universal"} data-cf-source-section-id={"intro"}>
        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"More than one billion people now use AI, according to DataReportal\u2019s global analysis. That is the clearest headline answer to the question, \u201chow many people use artificial intelligence?\u201d It shows that AI has moved well beyond a niche tool for researchers, engineers and early adopters."}</p>
        <p>{"But the number needs context. A large global user base does not mean most people use AI often, use it knowingly, or have equal access to it. Other commentary on global adoption points to the opposite side of the story: many people have still never had a direct, meaningful interaction with AI. For Australian AI builders, that gap matters. The opportunity is not only that AI is already big. It is that adoption is still uneven, and many practical use cases are still waiting to be built."}</p>
        <div data-cf-component-id={"image:intro"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Hero image"} data-cf-source-section-id={"intro"}>
        <ArticleImageBlock
          src={HERO_IMAGE}
          alt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
          caption="How many people use artificial intelligence? Over 1B."
          width={1600}
          height={1067}
        />
        </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-type={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-label={"Who is this guide for?"}>
          <AudienceGrid
            heading="Who is this guide for?"
            cards={[
            {
              title: 'Founders & Builders',
              description: 'For operators validating demand, pitching a vision, and moving before momentum stalls.',
              icon: <RocketLaunchIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'orange',
            },
            {
              title: 'Students & Switchers',
              description: 'For readers learning how strong technical partners evaluate traction, skills, and fit.',
              icon: <AcademicCapIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'purple',
            },
            {
              title: 'Community Builders',
              description: 'For connectors, mentors, and organisers helping founders meet collaborators in the right rooms.',
              icon: <UsersIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'yellow',
            },
            ]}
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:key-insight"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Key insight"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Key insight" variant="purple">
            {"The supplied sources do not provide a reliable Australia-specific percentage. The article therefore focuses on global estimates and practical implications for Australian builders."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>
        <div id="global-ai-user-estimate" data-cf-component-id={"section:global-ai-user-estimate"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"More than one billion people use AI"} data-cf-source-section-id={"global-ai-user-estimate"}>
          <h2>{"More than one billion people use AI"}</h2>
          <p>{"The clearest current answer is that more than one billion people use AI. That distinction matters because AI use is spread across many tools, apps and platforms, and not every provider publishes comparable user numbers."}</p>
          <p>{"ChatGPT launched to the public only a few years ago, yet AI has already become a mainstream digital topic. Platform milestones help show momentum, but they do not tell the whole story. One person may use several AI products, while another may use AI through a search engine or app without thinking of it as a separate AI platform."}</p>
          <p>{"So the best reading is simple: global AI use has passed the one billion person mark, but the precise count is still hard to measure. For founders, builders and community leaders, that means the more useful question is not only how many people use artificial intelligence, but how quickly everyday expectations are changing around it."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:global-ai-user-estimate"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: More than one billion people use AI"} data-cf-source-section-id={"global-ai-user-estimate"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-cd9c5821-ba76-4108-a04b-753fa3e1105a.jpg?alt=media&token=a2d218f2-bc07-42a1-8249-f90a137a16dd"
            alt="Hands using an AI chatbot on a laptop beside notes and coffee, reflecting everyday AI use at massive scale"
            caption="More than one billion people use AI"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <ArticleCallout title="" variant="brand">
            {"More than one billion people now use AI, according to DataReportal, but the number should be treated as a broad global estimate rather than a precise headcount."}
          </ArticleCallout>
        </div>
        <div id="why-ai-usage-is-hard-to-count" data-cf-component-id={"section:why-ai-usage-is-hard-to-count"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Why the real number is hard to measure"} data-cf-source-section-id={"why-ai-usage-is-hard-to-count"}>
          <h2>{"Why the real number is hard to measure"}</h2>
          <p>{"AI usage is hard to count because not every statistic is measuring the same thing. Some reports focus on people who directly use AI platforms, such as chatbots or image tools. Others focus on AI inside products and services that people already use. DataReportal notes that platform milestones exist, but there are still few evidence-based figures for total AI use across all tools."}</p>
          <p>{"Business adoption adds another layer. A company may say it uses AI, but that does not mean every employee uses it every day. Hostinger frames the business question as how widely companies use AI across industries, regions and functions. That is different from counting individual users. For readers, the safest approach is to ask what the number is really counting before comparing one AI usage claim with another."}</p>
          <h3>{"Three checks before trusting an AI usage statistic"}</h3>
          <p>{"First, define the user. A person actively prompting an AI tool is not the same as a person exposed to an AI feature inside search, software or a workplace system. Second, check the time period. Third, separate individual usage from company adoption. A business adoption figure can show organisational interest, but it should not be read as a direct count of people using AI."}</p>
        </div>
        <div id="global-adoption-gap" data-cf-component-id={"section:global-adoption-gap"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"The adoption gap is still the bigger story"} data-cf-source-section-id={"global-adoption-gap"}>
          <h2>{"The adoption gap is still the bigger story"}</h2>
          <p>{"The headline number is important: DataReportal argues that more than 1 billion people now use AI. But that does not mean AI has reached most people in a useful way. Another estimate claims that 84% of humanity, or about 6.8 billion people, has never interacted with AI in any meaningful sense. That figure should be read carefully, but it is a useful counterweight to the excitement around fast-growing tools."}</p>
          <p>{"AI can have a billion users and still feel distant, invisible or irrelevant to many communities. It is also about language, price, trust, device access, local context and whether the tool fits a real workflow. It is also a product signal. The next useful AI products may come from teams that understand a specific community deeply, rather than teams that only optimise for early adopters."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:global-adoption-gap"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: The adoption gap is still the bigger story"} data-cf-source-section-id={"global-adoption-gap"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-02c982f4-0b76-4dea-baea-d146ec9c46cb.jpg?alt=media&token=18e4c21f-8ffd-4f26-a5b5-3966a1278a2d"

        <div data-cf-component-id={"step-list:practical-next-steps"} data-cf-component-type={"step-list"} data-cf-component-label={"Practical next steps"}>
          <ArticleStepList
            title="Practical next steps"
            steps={[
            "More than one billion people use AI, but the exact global count is uncertain.",

        <div id="business-and-startup-implications" data-cf-component-id={"section:business-and-startup-implications"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What AI adoption means for Australian builders"} data-cf-source-section-id={"business-and-startup-implications"}>
          <h2>{"What AI adoption means for Australian builders"}</h2>
          <p>{"For Australian builders, the main lesson is that AI adoption is real, but uneven. Business research describes AI as moving beyond theory into day-to-day operations, with adoption varying by company size, region, industry and function. That means a startup should not assume every customer is already confident with AI. Some buyers may be testing tools, while others may still need help understanding where AI fits in a normal workflow."}</p>
          <p>{"The best early opportunities are often narrow, high-friction tasks where AI can show value quickly, such as reducing repetitive work or helping teams make a routine process faster. For students and AI professionals, the same pattern matters: useful AI skills are not only model skills, but also the ability to explain, test and apply AI in real business settings."}</p>
          <p>{"MLAI\u2019s role in the Australian ecosystem fits this moment. Events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools can help builders test ideas with people who see different parts of the market. That matters when adoption is uneven. A product that sounds obvious to one AI-fluent team may need a clearer use case, simpler setup or stronger proof point for another."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:business-and-startup-implications"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: What AI adoption means for Australian builders"} data-cf-source-section-id={"business-and-startup-implications"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-6a64b290-9599-4ee2-89ad-8c125b9adec4.jpg?alt=media&token=7106b571-6fa8-4dbb-9057-b7100fc46007"
            alt="Builder\u2019s hands using an AI estimating app on a dusty worksite tablet in Australia"
